data.title: Uranium Conversion Capacity Set For Major Expansion
data.description:
By Steffan Szumowski

Investors tracking the nuclear renaissance have focused on reactor development, data center power demand, and geopolitical fuel security. Yet, one critical link in the nuclear fuel cycle - the conversion stage - has long represented a domestic vulnerability for the United States. This step converts uranium ore concentrate, known as yellowcake or U3O8, into uranium hexafluoride, or UF6, the gaseous form required for enrichment into low enriched uranium (LEU) or high-assay LEU (HALEU).

Today, the Western world relies on just three primary commercial uranium conversion facilities: Cameco’s (CCJ) operations in Canada, Solstice Advanced Materials’ (SOLS) Metropolis Works in the United States, and Orano’s (private) facilities in France. This concentrated capacity has created a clear chokepoint as nuclear energy generation ambitions rise.

Recent announcements from Solstice and two new companies signal accelerating progress toward expanded and diversified domestic UF6 production capacity. These moves directly support U.S. policy goals of energy independence and supply chain resilience, while opening new investment opportunities across the nuclear value chain.

Solstice scales their existing facility

Solstice operates the only commercial uranium conversion plant in the U.S. at Metropolis Works in Illinois. Originally a Honeywell asset before the late 2025 spin-off, the facility was idled from 2017 to 2023 amid soft market conditions but restarted in 2023. In February 2026, Solstice announced a significant production ramp. The site is now projected to deliver over 10,000 metric tonnes of UF6 in 2026, a 20% increase over 2024 output.

The expansion is underpinned by a backlog exceeding $2 billion in orders, largely from domestic utilities, plus targeted Department of Energy support. Solstice has also retained an engineering, procurement, and construction firm to evaluate further capacity additions, positioning Metropolis Works to play an even larger role in meeting near-term demand.

Uranium Energy Corp. pursues vertical integration

Uranium Energy Corp. (UEC), one of America’s domestic uranium producers with licensed mines across Wyoming and South Texas, is taking a more ambitious step. In September 2025, UEC launched a wholly owned subsidiary, Uranium Refining and Conversion Corp. (UR&C), to develop a new UF6 conversion facility.

Recently, UR&C secured an initial regulatory milestone when the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission issued a docket number for the proposed plant. The facility is designed for approximately 10,000 metric tonnes of uranium per year as UF6, representing a substantial share of current U.S. annual demand of roughly 18,000 metric tonnes. UEC is collaborating with Fluor (FLR) on engineering and design while evaluating multiple potential sites. Once operational, the project would make UEC the only American company with full vertical integration from mining through conversion.

FluxPoint Energy Enters with a Greenfield Project

FluxPoint Energy, a private company only recently emerging from stealth mode, officially launched plans to build what would be the first new U.S. uranium conversion facility in nearly 70 years. Targeting first production in 2030 or 2031, the company is already advancing development with a secured site and front-end engineering studies underway.

Like the UEC project, FluxPoint is designing for substantial scale, initially 2,500 metric tonnes per year, with potential expansion to 10,000 metric tonnes per year as demand materializes. The company explicitly frames the initiative as restoring U.S. fuel sovereignty and eliminating an unacceptable chokepoint in the supply chain.

These expansions and new projects directly address the current U.S. annual UF6 conversion demand. By expanding domestic capacity, they insulate the American fuel cycle from import reliance and geopolitical risks. Significant amounts of federal support also exist in the form of executive orders to reinvigorate the domestic fuel chain and the federal-state partnership program for nuclear lifecycle innovation campuses. Higher conversion capacity will support both the existing reactor fleet and the deployment of advanced reactors that require reliable LEU and HALEU supply.

For investors, the developments translate into tangible opportunities across the nuclear value chain, from uranium producers to engineering and services firms.

CCJ, SOLS, and UEC are all constituents of the VettaFi Nuclear Renaissance Index (NUKZX) as shown below. NUKZX includes companies across the nuclear value chain, from fuel to utilities. NUKZX is the underlying index for the Range Nuclear Renaissance Index ETF (NUKZ).

It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

data.title: Buy IWS And XMLV For Midcap Value And Defense
data.description:
Introduction

I believe that 2026 could be the last year where we have positive returns in the market, as defined by the S&P 500, until 2030. That is less of a prediction of actual returns and more of a prediction of the potential downward pressures we could face. I wrote about this topic at the end of last year.

Despite that, I still feel there is money to be made with a strategic investment plan. During 2000-2002, when the S&P 500 posted three consecutive years of losses after the bursting of the tech bubble, midcaps and small caps managed to generate investment profits. Knowing this, I have been on the hunt for midcap indices that would work nicely for the investor who wants to stay fully invested in equity markets. My sojourn led me to the Russell Midcap Value-Defensive Index. Sadly, it does not have a linked ETF, mutual fund, or unit investment trust. Because of that, I had to find a way to mimic this index with two proxy ETFs.

The Russell Midcap Value-Defensive Index

The Russell Midcap Value-Defensive Index uses a multi-factor method to select stocks that are classified as value stocks and also rank highly as defensive investments.

FTSE Russell is very opaque about which components qualify for this index, but we do have data from 1996 to the present to at least measure the viability of this methodology.

This is a wonderful index if one is looking to participate in up markets (14.59% v. 17.86%) while also controlling tail risk in down markets (-2.88% v. -15.27%). It also beats the S&P 500 in my four favorite measures. It does a better job at beating the risk-free rate (0.63 v. 0.56). It generates a more reliable return (0.72 v. 0.63) and is also more consistent at outperforming inflation (0.62 v. 0.55). Also, any investment that generates a strong alpha (2.45%) is worthy of one’s investment dollars. If there was an available ETF, I would give this process a full-throated Strong Buy rating.

Faking It

Since no ETF is available, I will instead use two that cover both styles. For my value sleeve, I will explore the iShares Russell Midcap Value ETF (ARCX: IWS), and for my defensive sleeve, I will incorporate the Invesco S&P Midcap Low Volatility ETF (ARCX: XMLV). This gives me a two-pronged approach to mirror what I might get from a single ETF if it were available.

IWS uses the following methodology, which is based on the Russell Midcap Value Index:

IWS receives a 3-Star (Bronze) Morningstar rating. Meanwhile, Lipper Leaders gives it an average rating of 3.8/5 (Quintile 2, 28th Percentile). Zacks says it is a Buy, and TipRanks gives it a neutral 6/10 score. Seeking Alpha Quant says IWS is a Hold.

XMLV follows this algorithm, based on the S&P Midcap 400 Low Volatility Index:

Morningstar gives XMLV a better 4-star (Neutral) rating, yet Lipper Leaders gives it a more tepid 3.4/5 (Quintile 3, 49th percentile) score. TipRanks gives XMLV the same 6/10 assessment. Seeking Alpha Quant says it is a Sell.

By using these two ETFs together, I have a well-allocated portion for my midcaps, with these companies as my top holdings. Please note the absence of expensive technology companies.

data.title: Microsoft: Double-Digit Growth At A Rare Discount
data.description:
Over the past 6 months, Microsoft (MSFT) lost nearly $800 billion in market cap. I was bullish, but surely enough, short-term catalysts didn't play out as I had expected. But did it change my long-term thesis? No, not really. Yes, the stock dropped to a multi-year low valuation, but that seems like a rare opportunity in my honest opinion.

I maintained my rating as a Strong Buy despite the sell-off. And here are arguments for my bullish thesis.

Bearish Narrative Around AI Disruption Feels Overdone

For many years the market loved software and technology companies because they were asset light and operated at high margins. Now with the capex spending in place investors feel concerned. This arises not only from somewhat irrational spending estimates, but I also think the market tries to price in the risk of AI replacing some of the software companies.

It's fascinating seeing expanded tech-software ETF (IGV) dropping 31% over the past 6 months. Does it make much sense? I am not sure about that. Of course, the threat tied to AI capabilities should not be minimized in my opinion. Anthropic and OpenAI remain at the forefront of agentic revolution, but I also feel that the market may be too early pricing in a huge AI takeover. The software companies, such as ServiceNow (NOW), have been operating for years and have long-term relationships with businesses. They reported an all-time high of customers with above $5 million in annual contract value. What does it tell me? That software businesses are sticky and it's not an overnight process to replace them with AI. Sure, threat is real, multiples were elevated and I think they needed to contract from significant levels, but at the moment it feels like the market may have run ahead of AI scare. And I think this reduced risk appetite and slightly pressured sentiment around mega cap names.

Now how does this affect Microsoft? Paradoxically, it has exposure to OpenAI, so one could think concerns tied to AI disruption should be beneficial for MSFT stock. It wasn't the case so far. I think some of the investors assume OpenAI may be losing its competitive edge to Anthropic. That's surely a risk. Others could assume that AI capabilities may severely influence MS Office products and it causes a long-term risk for this technology's heavyweight. I am not in this camp. Why?

So far, MSFT has been successfully integrating AI through Copilot. It has significant moat and its legacy products are still widely used across organizations. Teams is used for internal communication, Outlook for e-mails, while MS Office products remain highly integrated with macros and were used for decades. Now AI enables synergy across different environments, and I just don't think it's that easy to replace Microsoft. Plus, don't forget about human resources as experience with MSFT's products has been a significant hiring edge for years. On top of all of this, we shouldn't forget that MSFT is massively investing in AI and is trying to be at the forefront of this revolution.

Microsoft stated its cash flow from operations totaled nearly $36 billion last quarter. Even if we account for high capital expenditure spending, free cash flow was still almost $6 billion. What I want to say is that it's an expensive pivot for MSFT, but the company has financial means to risk. Quite frankly, losing AI leadership could cost more than the current investments for this blue chip company in my honest opinion.

What's also interesting is that Microsoft is not the only Mag 7 name spending a lot. Multiple names reported an increase in capex for 2026. Now it makes me wonder. If the market trusted these companies for years, why not trust now? I understand that spending is significant, but I don't think there's anyone knowing the AI capabilities better. In other words, I wouldn't expect one of the best companies in the world to spend that much if they were not sure whether this will result in prosperity.

So, yes, the bearish narrative remains. I also understand why investors could feel concerned. It's a pricey pivot. But despite the concerns, these companies are raising capex spending estimates, and I think that's for a reason.

Earnings multiple has dropped meaningfully over the past 6 months. The benchmark now is trading at roughly 20x. Of course, this was not solely caused by AI theme, but it surely made a dent. But I think that's overdone. To me it feels irrational because if Mag 7 decides to pause or reduce capex spending, I don't think the market would love that. It may feel that spending was excessive, irrational, and companies are taking a step back. If they were to continue expanding capex, it may feel for the market difficult to comprehend the rationale behind. So, my point is that this status is likely to change. I favor upside due to trusting management of these companies. And especially MSFT which has been delivering quarter after quarter.

Double-Edged Sword of OpenAI

Microsoft dropped 28% over the past 6 months and is the worst performer among Mag 7. I think the reason behind is OpenAI. MSFT owns nearly 27% of this technology company. And at this point it feels like a double-edged sword to me.

There are two arguments I'd love to present. Firstly, as I pointed above, there's a risk of OpenAI losing a technological battle to Anthropic. While AI could become significant market, in the first innings a winner may take it all. The fear of the risk matters the most and weighs on the sentiment in my opinion. This could also be the case why Microsoft dropped the most over the past 6 months.

Another important argument I'd love to present is tied to commercial RPO commitments. Management announced that approximately 45% of this metric is committed by OpenAI, and it's not a small sum. About $625 billion. Why it's worrying? OpenAI remains a private company and the market doesn't have much transparency. So I think that's the reason why investors discount this and are not rushing to price in. However, if these RPO's materialize, that strongly supports the bullish thesis in my honest opinion.

Now I think the pressure may be largely resolved if OpenAI decides to go public. It would eliminate uncertainty factor and clarity is something the market loves. Also, as I pointed above, AI theme could become significant. Researchers estimate the global AI market could reach nearly $3.5 trillion by 2033. I believe if that were to happen, there's a high chance multiple companies would need to support the demand.

So, in the short-term I wouldn't be surprised if OpenAI continues weighing on the sentiment. But I honestly believe that in the longer term this may turn out to be beneficial for the mega cap name. Time will tell.

Stability In Performance Raises Confidence

I think has management done phenomenal job so far running the business. Its ability to stably perform quarter after quarter is something I love and adore. I also think the market underestimates it. I believe MSFT deserves a premium multiple, which is not the case at the moment.

Microsoft's earnings history is pristine. The recent performance indicates strong beats and market dominance. It also gives confidence in future analysts' guidance. The market anticipates at least double-digit growth for both top and bottom-line over the next three years. I'd not argue with that.

The company reported strong Q2 2026 results across its core segments. The revenue surged 17% on a year-over-year basis while bottom-line increased by 60% over the same period. This feels nothing but a home run. What I'd also love to point out is that MSFT continues operating at significant margins despite ongoing capex spending. It supports my bullish thesis.

Microsoft is expected to report Q3 2026 over the next few weeks. It could be a pivotal moment for its stock price. It approached oversold levels recently. Could it bounce if results were to come better than anticipated? Sure. But I think there will be a few metrics dictating price reactions. I'd say investors should keep an eye on future capex estimates. I am not sure whether the market would love a significant bump in the anticipated spending. Personally, I will be monitoring if MSFT can continue operating at elevated margins. If that's the case, it will raise my confidence in its outlook. I'd love to see the bottom line continue growing at a double-digit pace, alongside top-line number.

One more thing I feel investors could undermine and overlook is the continued share buyback program. Now management said they returned $6 billion through buybacks last quarter, but given its recent poor stock price performance, I'd love to see a higher number. It would also be great if they announced that they expect more shares to be repurchased in the future. This would support bottom-line growth in months to come. And, quite frankly, strengthen bullish outlook.

Microsoft Is Cheapest It's Been For Years

That's right. The technology heavyweight is currently trading at roughly 22x earnings multiple. If that doesn't sound cheap, keep in mind it's only a 4% premium versus the sector median at 21.5x.

To put it into perspective, Microsoft delivered 17% revenue growth over the past year. That's 70% outperformance versus peers at 10%. To me it appears that MSFT is undervalued and deserves premium valuation. I'd also love to point out that MSFT posted 29% bottom-line growth over the same period, a 2x advantage versus the industry's average of 15%. It's not expensive stock in terms of its performance.

Microsoft is also the third cheapest Mag 7 stock. I've excluded Tesla due to sky-high, triple-digit forward P/E. So, in terms of the most dominant companies, it's also not expensive.

What I love about this name is its margins. MSFT operates at above industry's gross profit margin (TTM) of 69% versus peers at 49%. Quite frankly, the net income margin (TTM) of 39% outperforms the sector median by 7x at 5%. I'd like to remind you that Microsoft is only trading at a 4% premium versus the sector median although it has meaningful advantage in regard to margins and pricing power. That's a strong argument for bullish thesis in my honest opinion. But even more than that, MSFT is able to deliver such performance despite high capex spending. That's why I feel the bearish AI narrative is overdone.

It's also good that MSFT isn't too leveraged. That's important given the fact capex isn't expected to slow down in months to come.

I said that Microsoft has financial means to take this risk. And to support this, the mega cap name delivered 28% in operating cash flow growth over the past year. That's a 75% advantage versus peers at 16%. So, if anyone could handle such spending, MSFT is a great candidate for it.

Risks Are Real, Too

We shouldn't forget that risks and concerns exist. I could also be wrong and there are aspects that investors need to take into account. So although I feel bullish, I'd love to point risks that could impact the outlook.

Now, I stated that the bearish AI narrative feels overdone. However, could the concerns continue? Sure, absolutely. If heavy capex will not turn into significant top and bottom-line growth for Mag 7, investors could become really worried. If that were to happen, I honestly wouldn't be surprised by further downside and multiple contractions.

There's one more thing I'd like to touch on AI theme. Microsoft has a significant exposure to OpenAI and Anthropic's substantial technological edge over competition could become an issue. If the market views OpenAI as the loser in the field, MSFT stock price could take a hit.

Investors should also understand that macroeconomic concerns and the conflict in the Middle East could pressure the stocks further. I am not in the camp this will be a prolonged conflict, I hope it will be resolved soon. But elevated oil prices and inflation concerns could become a trouble.

Don't forget that the market tends to climb up the wall of worries. Of course, these are valid risks and concerns, but I think MSFT may be due for a rebound.

Stability And High Performance At Discount

Well, I found the company interesting at 27x earnings multiple. Now that it trades at 22x forward P/E, to me it seems irrational market behavior that I believe won't last long.

I maintained my rating at Strong Buy. There are obvious risks, but given 6 months decline in the stock price, I think the market may already have priced in worries. Simply put, the sell-off seems overdone. I will be looking forward to seeing whether my expectations play out.

Wall Street analysts forecast a $587 price target. That's a 57% upside. It resonates with my bullish thesis and I definitely think it's a possible target in years to come.

data.title: What Versant's StockStory Deal Says About Life Beyond Pay-TV
data.description:
Versant (VSNT) started life as a public company as many spinoffs do: the share price tends to dip as investors that receive shares and do not want or can't hold them sell. There is usually a stabilization or recovery as a new shareholder base is found.

After a roughly 40% drawdown, shares appear to be recovering. The more important question is whether Versant has a long-term strategy to offset the structural decline in pay-TV subscribers. The recent StockStory acquisition offers a window into how the company plans to move beyond pay-TV.

Can Growth Areas Offset Legacy Decline?

Versant is following a different strategy from that attempted by Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) and Paramount Skydance (PSKY) of launching a massive streaming operation to get its content directly to viewers. This famously resulted in the "streaming wars," where costs surged and these segments had poor economics.

Versant appears to be applying a more targeted strategy, and probably a smarter one. It is extending its strongest brands vertically. Brands such as CNBC, MS NOW, Golf Channel/GolfNow, and Fandango are becoming more digital. This means more digital products, subscriptions, tools, software, and free ad-supported distribution. The StockStory acquisition fits with this strategy, as it will add insights and AI functionality to CNBC.

While the strategy makes sense, it is unlikely to reverse the revenue decline trend. At least not in the next one or two years. Revenue declined 5% in 2025 compared to the previous year, and guidance is for a mid-single-digit decline this year too. The growth engines need to become a much larger part of the revenue mix before the business can contemplate a turnaround. This is likely why CEO Mark Lazarus said during the most recent earnings call that they view revenue mix as a "critical indicator":

[...] view revenue mix as a critical indicator of our strategic transformation. In 2024, 17% of our revenue came from non-pay TV platforms. In 2025, that increased to 19%, and that was achieved without the benefit of the new initiatives launching this year. Our target is 33% over the next 3 to 5 years and over time to get closer to 50%, positioning Versant to a platform for growth over time.

International Growth Prospects

In addition to the digital products and platforms, there is some growth potential in international markets. One example is the golf platforms. According to management, users have booked over 40 million tee times across more than 9,000 courses globally, with international expansion still in an early phase.

A second area with international growth potential is its ticketing and movie software business. Versant owns Fandango and Rotten Tomatoes and recently purchased UK-based INDY. This acquisition gives Versant a foothold in the European market to sell points-of-sale, loyalty programs, and other movie theater software. CNBC is also becoming a more international brand. There is, however, a meaningful caveat in the 10-K. The CNBC brand was initially licensed for five years, and while it can be renewed, there is a risk restrictions could be placed if it were to "compete with certain Comcast businesses in certain international territories for a period after the expiration of the license."

M&A Strategy

Versant's M&A strategy looks disciplined so far. Versant is mostly doing bolt-on acquisitions that integrate well with existing assets and offer expansion potential. In addition to INDY and StockStory, Versant purchased Free TV Networks in January of this year, which expands its distribution capabilities.

The StockStory acquisition stands out as the most attractive so far, as it reinforces the capabilities of one of its best assets, CNBC. Adding AI recommendations and investment insights can increase the value of a CNBC subscription. The combination will have to be handled carefully, though, as it adds some regulatory risks.

Insider Transactions

Company insiders appear to truly see their stock as undervalued, as there have been some noteworthy purchases. Another sign of confidence in the stock is the Board's $1 billion share repurchase authorization.

Peer Comparison

A few legacy media players have managed the transition to a business model better suited to current realities.

The New York Times (NYT) is arguably the newspaper that best managed to adapt to the Internet era. It is delivering profitable growth again and has created some admired properties, such as its famous recommendation publication, Wirecutter. Fox (FOX) and News Corporation (NWS) also managed to defy the legacy media decline by reducing their reliance on advertising. Instead, they pivoted to high-margin subscriptions. In the case of FOX, they leaned heavily into live sports events. News Corporation pivoted its business model towards professional data and content licensing.

The success of these peers in adapting their business models suggests that Versant could potentially accomplish the same. Versant does have some powerful assets, with CNBC having the number one global position as a business media brand. The majority of its viewers tune in for news or sports, which are some of the most valued audiences by advertisers. However, as noted earlier, turning negative growth positive will take time and a much higher mix of revenue from the growth segments.

The central challenge for Versant is growth, as structurally it appears more efficient than peers. This is reflected in a net income margin that surpasses its peers and a gross margin that is close to that of News Corporation.

Despite the higher profit margins, investors are still penalizing Versant for its negative revenue growth. Its valuation multiples are a fraction of those of its peers.

Valuation

The valuation is clearly low, with its Price/Earnings ratio around 6x and its EV/EBITDA multiple at close to 3x. Shares also offer an attractive 4% dividend yield. If Versant can prove that it can reignite revenue growth, these multiples should rerate higher.

Risks

The main risk is straightforward: continued secular decline in linear distribution and ad revenue. Versant's own FY2026 guidance of negative revenue growth means the turnaround is still not in sight.

After the spin-off from Comcast (CMCSA), Versant also lost some of the leverage that came from negotiating inside a larger bundle. On top of that, the CNBC trademark-license arrangement adds a long-tail strategic complication, especially internationally.

Takeaway

The core question with Versant is whether the turnaround will succeed or if it is a melting ice cube of a legacy media company. It is encouraging to see management describe the mix of "growth revenue" as one of the most critical indicators they follow. Some strategic bolt-on acquisitions, like StockStory, can help accelerate the transition. Management seems to be taking sensible steps, but it is far from certain that Versant will join other peers that succeeded in transforming their legacy business models. Shares trade at very low multiples. However, a rerating is unlikely without evidence the turnaround is in sight.

data.title: ZipRecruiter: Facing Insurmountable Secular Headwinds
data.description:
This year, a risk-off attitude in the stock markets has ripped through small- and mid-cap stocks, especially tech companies that are considered at risk of a coming “SaaSpocalypse.” Amid this turnover in the stock market, many bargain opportunities exist, but stock selection is of the utmost importance: in my view, the challenging macro will also put many weaker companies out of business.

ZipRecruiter (ZIP), a hiring and jobs website, is one of those companies that faces severe disruption. Since the start of the year, ZipRecruiter has lost nearly two-thirds of its value, and unfortunately, I see more downside in this stock ahead.

I’m initiating ZipRecruiter at a "Sell" rating. In my view, this company faces a plethora of challenges: from potential technological disruption to unfavorable macro-driven hiring conditions, plus a strained balance sheet.

A marketplace for job recruiters

Let’s first kick off the discussion by offering a brief glimpse into what ZipRecruiter does. At its heart, ZipRecruiter is a two-sided marketplace for jobs. Companies, and specifically the recruiting and HR teams within companies, use ZipRecruiter to blast out their available job openings, while job seekers upload their resumes and profiles to the website. ZipRecruiter’s role, meanwhile, is to match candidates to open roles.

The snapshot below showcases how ZipRecruiter works for recruiters, which is the company’s exclusive source of revenue. Recruiters post their job openings on the site, while ZipRecruiter automatically finds candidate matches from the profiles on its site and invites them to apply. The company also purports to use AI to learn a particular client’s hiring practices over time, which is supposed to help with identifying closer matches.

The company’s revenue model charges commission against the recruiters that use the site. As shown in the chart below, ZipRecruiter deploys two different pricing models. In flat-rate pricing, ZipRecruiter uses a per-seat model to charge for users (recruiters) on the platform. In performance-based pricing, the company charges its clients based on clicks and responses, rather than a flat rate per seat.

Multiple years of decline and a sharp macro overhang

Let’s now dig deeper into the crux of the problem. The chart below showcases ZipRecruiter’s annual revenue and adjusted EBITDA trends, culminating in FY25 (the company recently reported fiscal Q4 results in late February).

It’s clear to see here that the company peaked during the COVID era, when businesses were hit with surprisingly rampant demand and staffed up teams to be able to prioritize growth. This was amplified by the fact that teams went remote during COVID, emphasizing the use of efficient, online recruiting tools. At its peak in 2025, ZipRecruiter generated $905 million of revenue at a 20% adjusted EBITDA margin. Since then, revenue has fallen precipitously, with 2025 revenue of $449 million (-5% y/y) not even attaining half of its prior record revenue.

We have to state the obvious here: with AI on the scene now, companies’ focus isn’t on hiring but on layoffs and efficiency. ZipRecruiter’s very premise may be at risk as businesses turn first to eliminating open roles and not backfilling positions that had natural attrition.

Quarterly trends exacerbate this issue. As shown in the chart below, the company’s count of employees using paid ZipRecruiter products fell by roughly 8k sequentially to 59.1k. Q4 revenue grew at only a 1% y/y pace.

The company is optimistic that macro-based hiring headwinds may fade in 2026, but I find this outcome unlikely. Per CEO Ian Siegel’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Following the sequential growth in Q2 and Q3 of 2025, Q4 '25 marked a return to year-over-year revenue growth. We achieved this milestone despite a challenging macroeconomic backdrop. That said, hiring demand in Q4 '25 was soft. Hiring demand dropped below what normal seasonality would have predicted and job openings declined 10% year-over-year. As a result, Q1 '26 started from a lower base of paid employers.

Our Q1 '26 revenue guidance of $106 million at the midpoint reflects this lower holiday baseline. However, in Q1 of '26, paid employer trends have rebounded year-to-date. Those trends are, in fact, stronger than the trends we called out as noteworthy in Q1 of '25. We are encouraged by the momentum we see in performance marketing revenue.

We should mention the issue of competition as well. Microsoft’s LinkedIn dominates across hiring platforms. It’s true that ZipRecruiter works across a number of platforms and channels and also has broad reach, but at the same time, many job seekers turn first to LinkedIn. We also think that as a software product, much of ZipRecruiter’s functionality (matching job requirements to candidates) can be easily overtaken by AI, especially when open marketplaces like LinkedIn already exist with reams of potential job seekers.

Heavy debt, upcoming credit line maturity

Another issue we have to be concerned about is ZipRecruiter’s balance sheet. As shown below, while we do note the company has a meaningful cash pile of $409 million, it is also shouldering a sizable $545 million of debt.

The company’s latest 10-K further details that this loan (with an original principal of $550 million) carries a modest 5% interest rate and will mature in 2030, a medium-term issue for the company.

We do note that the company’s line of credit, which is currently undrawn, will mature in April 2026.

Yes, ZipRecruiter does have a considerable cash balance to float its liquidity in the near term, which may not make this line of credit and its carrying/unused line fees necessary. But we are worried about tight credit markets overall, especially with the private credit industry facing sharp withdrawals over software industry exposure fears. With ZipRecruiter’s profitability waning, its leverage ratios will look more stretched, and the company will have a tough time raising additional capital to fund losses.

Valuation and key takeaways

For FY26, ZipRecruiter has issued what I believe to be quite rosy guidance: calling for flat y/y revenue (at $449.0 million in revenue) and at a 14% adjusted EBITDA margin that improves 5 points y/y, implying $62.9 million in adjusted EBITDA.

At current share prices near $2, ZipRecruiter trades at a $159.5 million market cap. After we net off the $409.1 million of cash against $544.8 million in debt on the company’s latest balance sheet, enterprise value goes to $295.2 million.

This positions the stock at 4.7x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA (if the company can actually achieve its target of flat y/y revenue, which to me is unlikely in a tough macro that is cutting jobs). Yes, on paper ZipRecruiter is cheap, but it’s a company facing secular headwinds: with shrinking corporate workforces, potential disruption from agentic AI-based tools that can displace ZipRecruiter’s functions, and a debt-laden balance sheet that may struggle to refinance amid tighter credit conditions, especially for smaller tech firms at risk for AI disruption.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Groupe Dynamite: Market May Be Underestimating Garage's UK Expansion
data.description:
That's life, my friends. Sitting and waiting too long is also a mistake in value investing.

As the Sage of Omaha Warren Buffett says, "It's far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price."

And that's precisely why I upgraded from Hold to Buy in Groupe Dynamite Inc. (GRGD:CA) earlier this year.

It was a reality check.

The growth wasn't going to stop anytime soon.

At the end of the day, this is a business putting up ~26.7% same-store growth, with low debt and very strong cash generation, around $300 million in FCF, a bit over a 3% yield, plus some optionality in the UK.

Compare them to any other apparel retailer and you'll see the difference.

I'm not kidding. You can pick any of them.

Zumiez (ZUMZ)? Dynamite’s gross margin is more than double. How about lululemon (LULU)? Also ahead, both on margins and growth.

One of the few that matches them on gross margin is Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF). But it's not even close on growth or cash generation because SG&A eats up a ton of dollars every year.

You would be buying the "Ferrari" of apparel retail for the price of a Porsche. They are definitely not trading at low multiples, but that makes perfect sense given what I've seen so far.

So after all that, let’s take a peek at how my Buy call did.

Right off the bat, you can see that it outperformed the S&P (SP500) by a large chunk.

Dynamite stock delivered just over 8% versus an S&P of almost -5%. We didn’t quite hit that ~$64 bottom people were talking about in March before earnings, but I’m pretty happy with how it’s played out so far.

And as I had pinpointed a price target of $85 for the short/medium term, I am only $5 away from that.

Looks like trusting them to beat expectations again—like they've done the past five quarters—paid off.

But what will happen from now on? I'll admit, I'm pretty excited to kick this off.

I've got full FY 2026 guidance in front of me, plus a few thoughts on the UK expansion and what that could mean from here.

A Quick Recap

I know, I know. What's already happened only gets you so far. It's like using yesterday's paper to predict what's next.

But, let's agree that a little context never hurt anyone.

I think it's less about the raw numbers and more about whether they beat their last guidance.

Last time we talked about this name, they were guiding for 25.5% to 27.7% same-store sales. They ended up right in the middle at 26.7% for FY 2025. Which is really very strong, but priced in.

What I didn't imagine was that same-store sales didn't slow down in Q4.

On the contrary. They skyrocketed 30.4%, well above the range for the whole year.

So, it seems to me that they went strong into FY 2026 even with all that slowdown narrative that you're probably hearing, driven by the K-shaped economy.

And it's actually funny. Many other chains are expanding rapidly by opening many stores. Dynamite is different. To give you an idea, they've opened "only" 9 stores, with the largest chunk of them being in the United States.

Here you can see the revenue swelling:

But you know what's even more important than that?

Well, if you thought of operational leverage, you hit the nail on the head. And what's interesting is that, unlike Abercrombie, Dynamite is actually offsetting some of that SG&A and pushing margins higher.

Looking at FY 2024, for example, SG&A was ~34% of sales and now it's only at 27.8%. This, combined with the higher gross margin, allowed them to expand their EBIT margin to 28.8%. Much higher than last year's 22%.

And here we come to the second forecast in the guidance.

I don't know if you remember, but management guided an adjusted EBITDA margin between 35% and 37%. They ended up pulling $477.9 million, just below the top of the estimates at 36.5%.

Capex came in at the low end, so FCF hit $335.2 million. They also still have $82.4 million sitting there ready to be returned.

It's practically the same thesis as before. The only difference is that it didn't slow down.

What to Expect from FY 2026?

For FY 2026, management has already given us some colors.

Of course, I imagine they might make some tweaks during the year (and they probably will), but for now it's like this:

Right off the bat, the first thing that caught my attention was the slowdown in same-store sales.

I'm not naive enough to think they would grow above 25% forever, and I already anticipated something similar along the way.

But don't get me wrong, ~11 to 14% is still quite strong.

If I pinpoint that growth would decrease from 36.7% to something closer to ~22 to 25%, we're talking about revenue close to $1.6 to $1.65 billion.

Another thing I noticed is that the adjusted EBITDA margin would stretch from 36.5% to 37.75–39.25%.

So, it doesn't seem like the margins have peaked.

For me, besides some gain in gross margin from pricing strategy (probably that move I told you about in their last article, turning them into 'luxury-inspired-premium') and a ton of operational leverage.

Just to give you the exact number, adjusted EBITDA would be between $620 and $650 million. A big jump from last year’s $477.9 million.

Now, to model OCF, I'm going to use an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion of ~75% (same as last year). This would give me an OCF of $358.4 million and, from the guided Capex of $105 million at the midpoint, FCF would be above $250 million.

Could I be underestimating their cash generation? Probably.

But since they are increasing Capex by a few million, I think this is the best approach here.

Optionality: UK Invasion

You hear a lot about the UK taking over the U.S., especially in music, but not much about Canada making a move on the UK.

It seems that changed this March.

And no, I'm not talking about Justin Bieber or anything like that.

Groupe Dynamite opened its first Garage stores in the UK this March and, judging by the way things are going, that's a great optionality for my Buy thesis here.

Just so you know (in case you want to stop by), there are two stores in upscale areas like Bluewater Shopping Centre in Kent and Oxford Street in London.

Here's a view of the stores before they became a Garage:

I think you understand the rationale behind choosing high DMA areas to continue generating the same or even more sales per square foot.

But what I think is most important here is trying to gauge the size of the opportunity for Dynamite. For example, these two openings are said to be the strongest the brand has ever had, across the U.S. and Canada.

So, perhaps modeling slightly higher sales per square foot makes sense here.

On top of those two, they’ve already locked in three more for FY 2026, two of them in Manchester.

And, since the UK is a good fashion showcase for Europe (except for France, I think), they could expand even further in the next three years if everything goes well.

Speaking of numbers, I'll start pulling some here.

As the stores are generating $952 per square foot in Q4. Their stores have 3,750 sq ft at midpoint, I can annualize AUVs of ~$3.6 million based on Q4 numbers.

I'll be a bit more conservative here and estimate something like ~$3.2 million per store since Q4 is peak season.

For stores in the UK, I imagine there will be a higher average ticket price. After all, these stores (especially the London one) are in high-end areas.

Perhaps something like $3.3 to $3.5 million in AUVs would be reasonable here.

Going a little further, let's imagine these stores operate with slightly lower EBITDA margins within the four walls than the group average in the first two years (perhaps between 22% and 28% in FY 2026 and FY 2027), converging to ~30% to 35% in FY 2028.

Here, the incremental growth is at least ~$6.6 million in revenue and ~$1.4 million in EBITDA in FY 2026. If they actually open these other three stores this year, that's just over $16 million annualized in sales and $3.6 million in EBITDA.

I think that perhaps in three years, these five stores will be generating almost $5 million in EBITDA.

In a base scenario, I imagine they could open somewhere between 20 and 25 stores in the UK by FY 2028.

Think about it.

They open just over 20 stores annually. If they repeat the openings from FY 2026 for at least three years, perhaps accelerating just a little more, they'll easily achieve that.

All of this would generate over $65 million annually in sales and almost $20 million in EBITDA in the worst-case scenario I can imagine (mature margins of ~30%). Basically 5% of the current EBITDA.

Still a "Buy"?

To be very direct with you, I've already given my base case before. Now I just need to cross-reference the data with Wall Street.

I told you that revenue should be between $1.6 and $1.65 billion for FY 2026.

Wall Street isn't far off that. They're pinpointing $1.61 billion.

For EBITDA, I'll stick with the $620 to $650 million range (guidance margins). And, for EPS, I can rely on Wall St.'s $3.05 projection growing ~30% year-over-year.

I can also look at their FY 2029 revenue projection ($2.12 billion) and already imagine an EBITDA margin of ~40% with the ramp-up done in the UK. Something close to an EBITDA of $850 million.

I think to start, I have to look at the PEG ratio.

Great for growth companies, by the way.

Since the stock is trading at a P/E of ~26.2x and EPS is projected to grow that 30% year-over-year, the PEG is something close to 0.87. Still good for a Buy.

But of course, EPS won't grow that much forever.

Assuming growth is something like the projected CAGR until FY 2029 (~24%) and putting that into Graham's Model, I arrive at a price target of ~$125 already applying a 25% margin of safety.

As the stock is trading at $80, with these assumptions in my model, the market seems to be pricing in EPS growth of ~10%.

That's lower than I would have predicted.

I think if they execute well in the UK and keep expanding across Europe, growth could stay strong for a few years. And the U.S. still looks a lot less saturated to them than Canada.

Let me look at the multiples again.

With my EBITDA projection and projecting an EV of $9.4 billion, the stock would be trading at a multiple of ~14.4 to 15.1x at the time I'm writing this.

If I plug in a multiple of 18 to 19x, based on peers like Aritzia (ATZ:CA) and the 5-year moving average of lululemon, I arrive at a price target of $100 to $108 for Dynamite.

An upside of ~25 to 35% from there.

I can also take the FY 2029 EBITDA projection ($850 million) and normalize the multiples to 16x, I find something close to $122.

Discounting this to present value at a cost of equity of 9%, the same as the last write-up, I arrive at something close to $95 per share.

For me, this remains a Buy with almost "free" optionality in Europe.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Inovio: 'Hold' As Upcoming PDUFA For INO-3107 Is Major Inflection Point
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Inovio Pharmaceuticals (INO) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Inovio: Back On Track With INO-3107 BLA For RRP Before End Of 2025." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company had a major issue in terms of having its Biologics License Application [BLA] of INO-3107 for the treatment of patients with Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis [RRP] filed in a timely manner. It was back in August of 2024 when it announced that it had to delay its BLA filing because of manufacturing issues. What I also mentioned was that it expected to complete its submission of its BLA of INO-3107 by the end of 2025 as well. I'm happy to report that the BLA submission was accepted by the FDA for review of this drug. The downside is that the last time I spoke about this company, I mentioned that it was seeking Accelerated Approval of this candidate so that it could have a quicker review period.

Despite the FDA accepting the BLA for review, it noted that adequate information hadn't been given for the FDA to grant the Accelerated Approval pathway for it. A Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of October 30, 2026, has been set by the FDA to review the drug and determine if INO-3107 should be approved for U.S. marketing for the treatment of patients with RRP. I had a "Hold" rating on this stock the last time around, and I believe it is important to keep this rating. Why is that? That's because if the company does achieve FDA approval of INO-3107, it would be a huge win for it. However, this regulatory review is a major inflection point for it. If it doesn't achieve U.S. marketing approval, not only would the stock price suffer, but the company could be forced to rely on another candidate in its pipeline by the name of INO-5412.

Not only that, but to extend its cash runway, it had to prioritize programs [cut other programs not focused on the BLA] and eliminate roles. Even with doing this, it was able to note cash runway guidance to Q4 of 2026. This still wasn't enough cash on hand anyways, and it had to raise $17.5 million in a public offering. As I will mention below, this cash raise won't carry it for long anyways. Based on the BLA of INO-3107 for the treatment of patients with RRP, plus the potential to develop INO-5412 with the PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific antibody cardonilimab with partner Akeso for the treatment of patients with Glioblastoma [GBM], I believe that this stock should be maintained with a "Hold" rating.

INO-3107 PDUFA Date For Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis Is Important For Company Going Forward

As I noted above, Inovio Pharmaceuticals had its Biologics License Application [BLA] of INO-3107 for the treatment of patients with recurrent respiratory papillomatosis [RRP] accepted by the FDA. The FDA did indeed end up accepting the BLA submission for review of this candidate for the treatment of these patients. It set a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of October 30, 2026, to review it by. Another positive to mention is that there was no mention that the FDA wanted to hold an advisory committee meeting to discuss the application. Where one negative comes into play with this BLA of INO-3107 is that while the company requested a review to take place under Accelerated Approval, the FDA didn't grant this. In the BLA file acceptance letter, it mentioned that adequate information was not submitted for the regulatory application to be considered under the Accelerated Approval pathway. Thus, for the time being, there is a standard 10-month review time, instead of the 6-month review time that could have been possible had the FDA granted the Accelerated Approval pathway review. With that being said, there is still a chance, albeit small, that it might be able to change the FDA's decision. The reason why is because it submitted additional documentation in February of 2026 to attempt to get the FDA to change the review from a standard one to an Accelerated Approval one.

Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis [RRP] is characterized as a disorder by which there is a growth of benign wart-like tumors, known as papillomas, growing in the respiratory tract of the patient. The main cause for the growth of these growths is that of Human Papillomavirus [HPV] types 6 and 11. What makes this challenging is the fact that despite patients receiving surgery for this, the growths tend to come back [recur]. The reason why is because even though surgery removes a lot of the tissue that has HPV, the virus persists in nearby tissues. Typically, the immune system is able to clear the virus, and thus most people don't get this. The problem arises when the immune system is compromised and is unable to clear the virus on its own, which then leads to the formation of these papillomas. Another item to mention is that what symptom a person might have depends upon where the growth itself happens. If it occurs in the vocal cords of a patient, then the person will have trouble speaking. If the growths occur in other parts of the respiratory tract, then a person would have trouble breathing. The bottom line here is that these patients must be screened constantly and must also go through many surgeries during a 1-year period. In addition, it is important to mention that there are two types of RRP, and they are:

  • Juvenile-onset recurrent respiratory papillomatosis - [JORRP] - RRP patients under the age of 5, also tend to have greater recurrence than those of AORRP
  • Adult-onset recurrent respiratory papillomatosis [AORRP] - Occurs above the age of 14

It is believed that there are 14,000 people in the United States with RRP [Inovio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #13]. Thus, there is a commercial opportunity for Inovio to go after. Why would INO-3107 be an ideal option for these patients? That's because surgery is not a good option. This option can lead to potential damage to the vocal cords of a patient for starters. Plus, a huge financial burden because of the cost impact of having surgery done. The role that INO-3107 plays is to elicit an antigen-specific T-cell response against HPV 6 and 11. The main point here is that there is a chance to eliminate the need for these patients to need surgery, as this candidate targets the underlying cause of RRP. Whereas, with surgery, it only involves removing infected tissue [with the virus remaining in other parts of tissue, and then RRP tends to recur a lot].

In order to determine if INO-3107 would be enough to help treat patients with RRP, it ran a phase 1/2 open-label study. The company did well with its therapy for sure because it accomplished what is important for these RRP patients, which is to see a reduction in surgeries. The company was able to show a 72% overall response rate [ORR] for the initial 12 month period [year 1]. The ORR number represents the patients who ended up achieving a complete response [CR] and a partial response [PR] as follows:

  • CR occurred when no surgeries were needed after having been given INO-3107 - achieved in 28% of patients [9 out of 32]
  • PR occurred when there was a ≥ 50% reduction and less than 100% in surgeries compared to previous year - achieved in 44% of patients [14 out of 32]

This data is pretty good, but what makes it more ideal is that as time went on, the therapy continued to work into year 2. That is, the ORR number went up to 86% by week 104 [Inovio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #17]. What I particularly like is that the CR rate increased from year 1 at 28% to 50% in year 2. Again, CR means that no surgeries were required.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Inovio Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $58.5 million as of December 31, 2025. As I mentioned above, the plan for the company is to specifically focus most of its resources on potentially getting INO-3107 for the treatment of patients with RRP to the finish line, towards potential FDA approval. To accomplish this, it had to prioritize programs and eliminate roles in the company as well. Speaking of eliminating roles, it reduced its headcount by 15%. Even with undertaking all of these items, it is still projected that its cash on hand will only be enough to fund its operations into Q4 of 2026. This wasn't going to be enough cash to carry the company forward, and thus it had to raise additional funds to keep itself well funded.

With that being said, it enacted a public offering to raise approximately $17.5 million. It is important to mention that this offering is still ongoing and is expected to close on or about April 6, 2026. In my opinion, it didn't extend its cash runway that much, though. Why is that? It is because it mentioned in its Q4 and full-year 2025 results that it burned approximately $22 million in cash in Q1 of 2026. Thus, I believe that in the next several months it is likely going to have to find a way to raise additional funds. The hope is that it could somehow develop a collaboration agreement for INO-3107 or another candidate from its pipeline to avoid dilution, but there is no assurance of this happening.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Inovio Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the expected PDUFA date review of October 30, 2026, by which the FDA will decide upon whether or not INO-3107 should be approved to treat patients with RRP. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the FDA will approve this therapy for the treatment of these patients. Especially since the BLA was made possible from a phase 1/2 open-label study. In addition, the company submitted additional information to the FDA in February of 2026 in order to see if it can get a meeting set up to discuss the potential of changing the ongoing PDUFA review from a standard timeline to an accelerated one. That is, to move the review of INO-3107 to an Accelerated Approval pathway. The risk here is that even with such a request, there is no guarantee that the FDA will find the additional information provided to be enough to warrant the Accelerated Approval pathway.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of competition that might be in place for INO-3107 should it be approved by the FDA for the treatment of patients with RRP. The reason why is because Precigen (PGEN) has already been granted U.S. marketing approval of PAPZIMEOS for the treatment of adults with RRP. To this end, it is the first and only FDA-approved HPV-specific immunotherapy for these patients. The point here is that even if Inovio is granted FDA approval of INO-3107 for these patients, there is no assurance that it will be able to compete well against PAPZIMEOS. What Inovio mentions and what it could do is potentially provide an advantage, which is no minimal residual disease [MRD] surgery during the dosing window. About 72% of complete responders with PAPZIMEOS [Inovio Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #22] had surgery in the dosing window.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that it is in. As I mentioned above, its $58.5 million in cash as of December 31, 2025, would only be enough to fund its operations into Q4 of 2026. Thus, it had to enact a cash raise of approximately $17.5 million. The thing is that despite having done this, it is still burning an excessive amount of cash per quarter [it burned through $22 million in cash in Q1 of 2026]. Thus, it is my belief that it is going to likely have to raise additional cash this year, especially as it gets closer to the PDUFA date of INO-3107 of October 30, 2026. The first risk here is that there is no assurance that it will be able to raise additional funds to keep its operations going. If it does find a way to raise additional funds, then it might dilute shareholders further and cause the stock price to trade lower. Where it could avoid all this is if management can deliver and make some type of a collaboration deal with either INO-3107 or another candidate from its pipeline.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be in terms of another candidate from its pipeline, which is INO-5412. This candidate is to be combined with collaboration partner Akeso's first-in-class PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific antibody for the treatment of patients with GBM in the phase 2 INSIGhT study. Dosing for this program is not expected to happen until the 2nd half of 2026. The risk here is that upon completion of this trial, there is no assurance that this combination is going to yield positive results for starters. Secondly, there is no guarantee that the data produced from this adaptive mid-stage study is going to be enough to merit the ability to move this program forward into a late-stage phase 3 study thereafter.

Conclusion

Inovio Pharmaceuticals did have a problem with its BLA filing of INO-3107 for RRP back in 2024. However, it was able to overcome the manufacturing issue delaying this BLA filing and was indeed eventually able to submit it. The FDA accepted the BLA back on December 29, 2025, for review and had set a PDUFA target action date of October 30, 2026. The downside is that the company wasn't yet able to convince the FDA to allow this program to be done under the Accelerated Approval pathway.

The company sent additional information in February of 2026, but even still, there is no assurance that the FDA will change its mind on this issue. I still believe that this company should remain as a "Hold" rating in the off chance it does receive FDA approval of INO-3107 for the treatment of patients with RRP. However, it definitely still has another risk involved that I highlighted above, which is its financials. It is my opinion that it might have to find a way to raise additional cash during 2026. Based on everything I mentioned above, I believe that this stock should remain as a "Hold" rating.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Shopify: Exploit Misinformed AI Selloff For 20% Annual Returns (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shopify (SHOP) is in a relatively rare 35% drawdown amid a broad risk-off trend in markets.

As I warned in late 2025, Shopify investors should have learned a lesson from the DoorDash (DASH) selloff, which exemplified how an impeccable company could still face a sharp drawdown if valuation is stretched too far.

Unlike DoorDash, though, in my view, Shopify's long-term trajectory remains one of the clearest, most predictable, and most attractive investment stories in the market.

I am upgrading Shopify to "Buy," with no change to my forecast that Shopify will become a $500 billion company by the early 2030s.

Shopify AI Concerns Cause Misinformed Selloff

Year-to-date, Shopify's stock has declined alongside the "AI Loser" basket. The only reason it's not as commonly mentioned as an AI loser is that it's still trading at a high valuation, especially relative to other names in that basket.

That said, Shopify hasn't been spared from investor AI concerns as well. Look no further than the recent earnings call, where almost every question from analysts raised some worry about agentic commerce or AI.

In my view, Shopify is the embodiment of AI worries going too far.

Starting with the fact that the fundamental "job-to-be-done" by Shopify is to make entrepreneurs' lives easier by letting them focus entirely on their products and services rather than the back-end of operations. Shopify's customers are exactly the ones who don't want to learn how to use Claude on their own, or vibe-code their way to an online storefront.

Then, it's about the fact that Shopify has arguably one of the most dense pools of talent in public markets, led by CEO & Founder Tobi Lutke, who's known for his leadership of an extraordinary culture. This is best reflected by the fact that Shopify's revenue per employee more than tripled since 2022, as revenues grew at a high-20% CAGR while headcount declined.

Lastly, it's about Shopify's ability to leverage AI. In fact, I would argue Shopify is one of the biggest beneficiaries of AI. With products like Sidekick (AI co-founder), SymGym (AI customer simulator), Tinker (AI tools suite), and, most tangibly, Shopify Agentic Storefronts, you can clearly see how AI should drive merchant and GMV acceleration for Shopify.

To me, it's only a matter of time before the market realizes that AI is actually going to push Shopify's growth trajectory above 30% for the foreseeable future.

Fundamentals Remain Impeccable, Except For Short-Sighted Margin Worries

Here's the simple truth: if you're not a long-term investor, I don't think you should go near Shopify. As we'll discuss in the next section, Shopify's near-term valuation levels are very high, so betting on the stock doing well over the short term is a mere bet on changing market sentiment.

As we've established that, let's discuss the only fundamental issue that could be raised by the bears.

Shopify is growing like a machine, with GMV growth accelerating to 31% in the recently announced Q4, surpassing $124 billion—no issues here:

Revenue growth is similarly strong, rising roughly alongside GMV:

However, double-clicking within revenues, the lower-margin and larger merchant solutions line is growing significantly faster than the high-margin subscriptions:

As a result, Shopify's gross margins are dropping:

And, while operating leverage continues to improve extraordinarily, it's still not enough to offset the gross margin contraction, leading to earnings growth slower than revenue growth:

So, what are the bears saying? That the elevated growth is coming at a price, and that's true, but here's where I think they are missing the point.

First, Shopify switched from 1-month free trials on subscriptions to three. This is a headwind to MRR growth that is set to end in Q2-26, at which point revenue growth is set to accelerate, and margins are likely to tick up.

Second, most of the lower-margin revenues are in relatively early innings (things like AI solutions, payments, capital, and advertising). With time and scale, Shopify is likely to deliver better economics on all of them. In my estimation, it's very likely that Q4 was close to a trough on gross margins, assuming no major macro headwinds.

I don't see a scenario where Shopify doesn't get back to 50% gross margins over the long term, which is why I argue the margin concerns are extremely short-sighted.

Shopify's Valuation Is Cheap, But Only From A Long-Term Perspective

In today's volatile markets, it's very difficult to maintain a long-term focus. For a company like Shopify, which is set to grow earnings at a 20%+ clip for the rest of this decade, it's obvious that, barring any major disruption, the stock will be much higher ten years from now.

My ten-year model projects a 29% CAGR in FCF per share and nearly 20% annual returns based on a 30x P/FCF exit multiple. Amazing.

However, from a near-term perspective, Shopify trades at 65 times forward earnings, and any change in sentiment could easily lead to a 20%+ selloff, just like the one we're experiencing right now.

So, what's the right play? In my view, investors first need to establish extreme conviction in Shopify's long-term prospects. Without such conviction, it will be very hard to hold the stock at lofty multiples during sharp downturns.

Then, it's about correctly sizing the position. If Shopify traded at 30 times earnings, it would probably be 20% of my portfolio. But, at 65x, caution is necessary, which is why it's only 5% of my portfolio, and this is an individual matter of portfolio composition and personal preferences.

Lastly, it's about smartly managing the position. Shopify is not the type of stock you want to buy aggressively at every price. You should look for dips and set a certain bar where you're going to add to your position. For me, that bar, right now, is below $120, where my projected annual returns are around 20%.

Conclusion

Shopify is one of the most attractive long-term stories in the market, with countless hyper-growth verticals, significant secular tailwinds, and an extraordinary founder-led management team.

Its near-term valuation is going to cause volatility and sharp selloffs occasionally, which is why I encourage short-term investors to refrain from investing in this name.

However, I find this to be one of the most predictable and resilient long-term 20% annual return investments in the market, and I view this AI-led selloff as a great buying opportunity.

As such, I am upgrading Shopify to a "Buy."

data.title: WTI Crude Oil Whipsawed Above 20-Day MA Ahead Of Trump's Speech And U.S.-Iran Ceasefire Hopes
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The West Texas oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) has staged the expected bullish move, where it cleared above $102.25 per barrel and hit a closing level of $112.84 last Thursday, April 2, 2026, before the Easter holiday.

Market participants continue to discount the "optimism of a ceasefire deal" between the US and Iran from US President Trump’s social media posts and public speeches made in the past week.

In today’s early Asian session (Monday, April 6, 2026), the price actions of the West Texas oil CFD gapped up by 2.9% to print a current intraday high of $116.17, just shy of its 4-year high of $119.54 recorded on March 9, 2026, as the market digested the possibilities of further hostilities between US and Iran where Trump posted a social media message with foul language, warning Iran’s power infrastructure will be destroyed if Tehran does not open the Strait of Hormuz by Tuesday, April 7, 2026 8.00 p.m. Eastern Time (an extension of an earlier Monday deadline).

Interestingly, the earlier gains of the West Texas oil CFD were all wiped out as it declined by 2.3% at $110.27 at the time of writing.

Ceasefire hopes are the primary driver of the current intraday whipsawing in oil prices. An Axios report stated that the US, Iran, and a group of regional mediators are discussing terms for a potential 45-day ceasefire that may lead to a permanent end to the war. In addition, US President Trump said he plans a news conference on Monday at 1.00 p.m. Eastern Time.

Prediction market is still expecting a US-Iran ceasefire in June 2026

The above chart reflects the market-implied probability of an official ceasefire agreement between the US and Iran on various specific dates from the prediction market platform, Polymarket, where participants trade contracts based on the probability of future events.

As of Monday, April 6, 2026, the probability of a ceasefire by April 30 remains low at 22.5%, while the likelihood rises significantly to 51.5% by June 30 (see Fig. 1).

Let’s now focus on the potential short-term trajectory (1-3 days) of WTI crude oil from a technical analysis perspective,

WTI Crude Oil - Minor bullish impulsive up sequence from the March 23, 2026 low remains intact

The West Texas oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) managed to stage a bullish breakout last Thursday, April 2, 2026, from a three-week sideways range configuration.

Hence, a potential minor bullish impulsive up-move sequence has kick-started from its March 23, 2026 low (see Fig. 2).

Watch the $102.25 short-term pivotal support to maintain the near-term bullish bias. A clearance above $116.56/119.54 sees the next intermediate resistances to come in at $124.40 and $131.30/132.67 (also close to a Fibonacci extension cluster).

However, failure to hold at $102.25 and an hourly close below it negates the bullish tone for another round of minor corrective pullback to retest the next intermediate support zone at $96.44/93.70 (also the 20-day moving average).

Key elements to support the near-term bearish bias on WTI crude oil

  • The price actions have continued to oscillate within a minor ascending channel in place since the March 23, 2026 low, with its channel support coming in at around $102.25.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator is still holding above its pullback support at around the 50 level.

data.title: Is NVIDIA 2026 A Better Value Than 'Liberation Day' 2025?
data.description:
NVIDIA could be comparable to 'Liberation Day' on a fundamental basis

NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ: NVDA), in conjunction with semiconductors as a whole and the software that relies on them, are having a bad years as sectors. As of now, it's nowhere near as bad as last April's 'Liberation Day' tariff tantrum meltdown, but NVIDIA is underperforming the market. The question here is, despite the growth in share price from April 2025 til now, does the stock look like as good a deal fundamentally as April 2025? Let's explore.

Analysts estimating NVIDIA forward earnings

The most reliable data here will be the next 3 years of earnings; beyond that, there are not enough analysts offering their opinions to make the data worthwhile. As for 2027 through 2029, high double-digit average earnings growth is expected; if true, this would be an extension of the already phenomenal run. With revenue and earnings growth being the two most important fundamental multiple points, this growth can make a stock as cheap as a prior meltdown with only half as steep a drawdown.

A Forward P/E Ratio Comparison

Here we can see the forward GAAP P/E ratio for NVIDIA. If you observe the dip for 2025, we are right at those same forward earnings multiple levels.

-30+% drawdown vs. -15+%

Looking at the drawdowns over this period, the current drawdown is less than half as much as the year prior, but earnings growth and growth expectations have made the stock as cheap from this perspective as it was last year.

3 Year price vs growth metrics

Taking a look at the trailing 3 year reflexivity patterns, or in other words, comparing the 3-year growth rates in revenue, earnings, and free cash flow to the growth in price, we now find price once again dipping below those growth rates. This is usually a positive marker and entry point if growth rates continue. These are famously used by both Peter Lynch and George Soros as the starting point in value analysis in their respective books on investing.

Catalysts in the works

CEO Jensen Huang does not cease to come up with new partnerships and catalysts. One of the first things that I found interesting was Omniverse robotics training. This initial partnership with Disney (DIS) to now have a walking, talking Olaf robot [from the movie Frozen] greeting guests around the Hong Kong and Paris properties can be seen in this NVIDIA keynote.

This new open computing platform is allowing teams to train robotics in virtual environments rather than physical ones. While this is a new business offering, I'm starting to see NVIDIA's willingness to begin stepping outside of strictly chip making and become a multi-revenue stream hyperscaler like the rest of the Mag 7. Companies like Google (GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN) are starting to compete on AI chips, thus it makes sense that NVIDIA would also begin to compete with their own computing and cloud computing businesses.

Huang has also expressed that NVIDIA will be involved in robo-taxis and other 'physical AI' programming. It would be amazing, for instance, if the Omniverse could one day train robo taxis in virtual environments using 3d models from Google earth and maps for instance. No more guys with cars covered in lidars and cameras endlessly driving around wasting gas and training models.

In the Olaf robot case, for instance, they observed that the robot was stomping his feet too hard. In a virtual environment, they were able to train the robot to soften its steps without physical training. This is smart, innovative thinking. Google's DeepMind is also involved in this type of training, and it could be the key to robotics exponentially improving in articulation and movement.

NVIDIA is framing the future of data centers in the same light as factories. This will be the new industrial infrastructure driving the future, in their opinion, and I tend to agree with the premise. With tokens being the units of how AI is tabulated for cost to the consumer, the demand for 'tokens' or access to inquiries by large language models and gen AI video models seems like it will only grow from here.

I mentioned this in a previous article titled:

Boss, More Work Please

The advent of AI agents is just now taking off with the gaining popularity of agentic work layers like Open Claw. This allows a user to run tasks like sending emails, coding, and paying for things when needed to complete a task, all autonomously. They use the popular LLMs as underlying engines, but work around the clock. These can get very expensive to run all the time, and data centers will benefit from agent usage that can output much more work than a human. While they will replace some human jobs, the remaining humans can put as many of these to work as they want, probably increasing the workloads and token revenue despite fewer workers.

In essence, these agents become the employees, and the boss pays for their tokens. Still, probably much less expensive than the same number of physical employees, but the data centers only care about the workload volume, not the number of human seats. While the seats are a great number to say, we have many more users, this is going to become old thinking. Eventually, I assume enterprise software companies and cloud platforms will begin to count the number of AI agents using their platforms, which is just as good, if not better than humans, due to their ability to output higher volumes of work.

NVIDIA is still at the center of all of this, and CUDA is the backbone of AI programming. There will be competitors along the way, but it seems like there is enough future cloud compute demand to go around. If what we hear from Amazon, Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOGL) is true, they can't build enough to meet the demand, even though the market is still questioning the capacity as being excessive. I tend to believe the leaders who run cloud computing, even though they are incentivized to make these statements.

Even though many are trying to reduce dependency on NVIDIA, NVIDIA themselves have the ability to reduce dependency on the end users by pursuing the same lines of business that their customers use their products in.

Balance sheet

I couldn't complete another NVIDIA article without revisiting their big, beautiful balance sheet. This is the best one you will find in semiconductors, in my opinion. They have a large net cash position with only $10.04 billion in long term debt and $62.56 billion in cash and short-term investments. With an EBITDA print of 133.23 billion TTM, this company not only sits on a lot of dry powder, but has immense borrowing capacity for future years if the EBITDA maintains its growth rates. They have the ability to build their own cloud business in the future that could be competitive with their customers if they so choose to.

Valuation

Peter Lynch is famous for using the PEG ratio in his analysis of growth stocks. This is simply the P/E divided by the growth rate in earnings. A ratio of 1 or less was a good deal. So a 20 P/E growing EPS at 20% is a good growth stock prospect. He also didn't chase anything with more than 25% growth assumptions, so I also set my cap there for any growth multiple. In this case, I will take the consensus 2027 EPS and put a 25 X multiple on it since the growth rate is expected to exceed this substantially.

With a 2027 $8.29 consensus, $8.29 X 25 would equal a $207 fair value using a 25 X upper limit. It could go much higher since the growth rates are expected to be high double digits the next two years, but it's better to be on the conservative side with typically cyclical sectors.

$207 is still well below the Wall St. average rating but quite a bit more than the current price.

Risks

US regulators are clamping down on everything AI as it relates to Chinese exports. Super Micro (SMCI) recently had executives indicted for smuggling server racks to China with advanced chips that needed licensing to be sold into China. NVIDIA can't really control where the racks go. Even though the purchaser may have been in the US, those racks can move anywhere afterward if the rack owner is daring enough to bring those to a barred country.

The competition from hyperscalers is also real, and I would worry about that more than Micron (MU) or AMD (AMD). Amazon and Google, in particular, have brilliant teams and even more robust balance sheets than NVIDIA.

Conclusion

NVIDIA, on a fundamental basis, is as cheap as liberation day 2025. If you think the growth rates projected over the next 3 years hold up, this is once again a strong buy.
